item.title: The Twisted Crimes of the Hillside Stranglers of Los Angeles
item.description:
Between 1977 and 1978, a series of killings in Los Angeles were linked to two cousins, Kenneth Bianchi and Angelo Buono. The victims, mostly young women, were abducted and later found in hillside areas, leading to the name “Hillside Stranglers.”
The case caused widespread fear as investigators worked through thousands of leads. Authorities later determined the pair used deception, including posing as police officers, to gain victims’ trust before the crimes.
Bianchi was arrested in 1979 and later testified against Buono as part of a plea deal. Both were convicted and received life sentences. The case remains one of the most well known serial crime investigations in Los Angeles history.

media:content.media:title: The Twisted Crimes of the Hillside Stranglers of Los Angeles
media:content.media:description:
Between 1977 and 1978, a series of killings in Los Angeles were linked to two cousins, Kenneth Bianchi and Angelo Buono. The victims, mostly young women, were abducted and later found in hillside areas, leading to the name “Hillside Stranglers.”
The case caused widespread fear as investigators worked through thousands of leads. Authorities later determined the pair used deception, including posing as police officers, to gain victims’ trust before the crimes.
Bianchi was arrested in 1979 and later testified against Buono as part of a plea deal. Both were convicted and received life sentences. The case remains one of the most well known serial crime investigations in Los Angeles history.

item.title: The $125 Million Candy Heiress Who Vanished Without a Trace
item.description:
Helen Brach, a wealthy widow tied to the Brach’s candy fortune, disappeared on February 17, 1977, after leaving a medical visit in Minnesota. She was never seen again and was later declared legally dead in 1984.
Investigators uncovered fraud schemes involving horse dealers linked to her finances, and one suspect was later convicted of defrauding her but not her disappearance. Despite decades of investigation, her body has never been found.

media:content.media:title: The $125 Million Candy Heiress Who Vanished Without a Trace
media:content.media:description:
Helen Brach, a wealthy widow tied to the Brach’s candy fortune, disappeared on February 17, 1977, after leaving a medical visit in Minnesota. She was never seen again and was later declared legally dead in 1984.
Investigators uncovered fraud schemes involving horse dealers linked to her finances, and one suspect was later convicted of defrauding her but not her disappearance. Despite decades of investigation, her body has never been found.
